name: env
context: Vite environment variables — .env files, VITE_ prefix for client exposure, import.meta.env built-ins, mode-based loading order, TypeScript IntelliSense via vite-env.d.ts
info: Vite exposes environment variables to client code through the `import.meta.env` object, which includes built-in constants (MODE, BASE_URL, PROD, DEV, SSR) and any user-defined variables prefixed with `VITE_`. The VITE_ prefix is a security boundary — only variables starting with VITE_ are bundled into client code, so secrets like database credentials or API keys without the prefix stay server-side and are never shipped to the browser. Variables are loaded from .env files in a specific precedence order: `.env` (base, always loaded), `.env.local` (local overrides, git-ignored), `.env.[mode]` (mode-specific, e.g. .env.production), `.env.[mode].local` (mode-specific local overrides, git-ignored), where mode-specific files take precedence over generic ones. The default mode is `development` for `vite dev` and `production` for `vite build`, but custom modes like `staging` can be set via the `--mode` flag. In [[vite/config]], use the `loadEnv(mode, envDir, prefixes)` helper from Vite to access env variables since `import.meta.env` is not available in the config file itself. Variable expansion is supported via dotenv-expand syntax (`VITE_FULL_URL=$PROTOCOL://$HOST`). For TypeScript IntelliSense on custom VITE_ variables, augment the `ImportMetaEnv` interface in a `vite-env.d.ts` (or `env.d.ts`) file referenced by tsconfig. In HTML files served by Vite, env variables can be injected using `%VITE_MY_VAR%` syntax. Note that `mode` and `NODE_ENV` are distinct concepts — mode controls which .env file loads while NODE_ENV controls framework behavior, though Vite sets NODE_ENV from the mode by default. Env variables are statically replaced at [[vite/build]] time, not looked up at runtime.
triggers: set up environment variables in vite
body:
# Vite Environment Variables & Modes Vite provides a complete system for managing environment variables across development, production, and custom modes. Variables are loaded from `.env` files, filtered by the `VITE_` prefix for security, and exposed to client code via `import.meta.env`. ## Built-in Environment Constants These are always available on `import.meta.env` without any configuration: | Constant | Type | Description | |----------|------|-------------| | `import.meta.env.MODE` | `string` | The current mode: `'development'`, `'production'`, or a custom mode | | `import.meta.env.BASE_URL` | `string` | The base URL the app is served from, set by the `base` config option in [[vite/config]] | | `import.meta.env.PROD` | `boolean` | `true` when `MODE === 'production'` | | `import.meta.env.DEV` | `boolean` | `true` when `MODE !== 'production'` (always the inverse of PROD) | | `import.meta.env.SSR` | `boolean` | `true` when running in the server-side rendering context | ```typescript // Usage in application code if (import.meta.env.DEV) { console.log('Running in development mode'); } const apiBase = import.meta.env.PROD ? 'https://api.example.com' : 'http://localhost:3001'; ``` ## .env Files — Format and Loading Order Vite uses [dotenv](https://github.com/motdotla/dotenv) to load environment variables from files in the project root (or the directory specified by `envDir` in [[vite/config]]). ### File Format ```bash # .env # Comments start with # VITE_APP_TITLE=My Application VITE_API_URL=https://api.example.com # Unquoted values are trimmed VITE_KEY=some-value # Quoted values preserve inner whitespace VITE_GREETING="Hello, World!" # Single quotes are also supported VITE_MESSAGE='This is a message' # Multi-line values with double quotes VITE_MULTILINE="line1 line2" ``` ### Loading Order (Highest to Lowest Priority) Mode-specific files take precedence over generic ones. Local files take precedence over non-local ones: | Priority | File | Purpose | Git-ignored? | |----------|------|---------|-------------| | 1 (highest) | `.env.[mode].local` | Mode-specific local overrides | Yes | | 2 | `.env.[mode]` | Mode-specific variables | No | | 3 | `.env.local` | Local overrides for all modes | Yes | | 4 (lowest) | `.env` | Base variables for all modes | No | All matching files are loaded and merged, with higher-priority files overriding lower ones. A variable defined in `.env.production.local` overrides the same variable in `.env`. ```bash # .env — shared across all modes VITE_APP_TITLE=My App VITE_API_VERSION=v2 # .env.development — dev-specific VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:3001 VITE_DEBUG=true # .env.production — production-specific VITE_API_URL=https://api.example.com VITE_DEBUG=false # .env.local — personal overrides (git-ignored) VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:8080 ``` **Important:** Files already existing at the time Vite starts are loaded. A variable that already exists in the environment (e.g., set via the shell) will NOT be overwritten by .env files. To reset all overrides, delete the `.env.local` file. ## The VITE_ Prefix — Security Boundary Only variables prefixed with `VITE_` are exposed to client-side code via `import.meta.env`. This is a critical security feature. ```bash # .env VITE_PUBLIC_API_KEY=pk_live_abc123 # Exposed to client — import.meta.env.VITE_PUBLIC_API_KEY DATABASE_URL=postgresql://secret # NOT exposed — only available server-side SECRET_API_KEY=sk_live_xyz789 # NOT exposed — stays server-side VITE_APP_NAME=MyApp # Exposed to client ``` ```typescript // In application code console.log(import.meta.env.VITE_PUBLIC_API_KEY); // 'pk_live_abc123' console.log(import.meta.env.DATABASE_URL); // undefined console.log(import.meta.env.SECRET_API_KEY); // undefined ``` **Security rules:** - Never put secret keys, tokens, or credentials in `VITE_` variables — they are embedded in the JavaScript bundle and visible to anyone who inspects the page source - Use `VITE_` only for values that are safe to be public: public API keys, app names, feature flags, API base URLs - Access secrets only in server-side code ([[vite/dev-server]] middleware, API routes, SSR handlers) via `process.env` The prefix can be customized via the `envPrefix` option in [[vite/config]]: ```typescript // vite.config.ts export default defineConfig({ envPrefix: ['VITE_', 'PUBLIC_'], // now PUBLIC_* vars are also exposed }); ``` **Warning:** Never set `envPrefix` to `''` (empty string) — this would expose ALL environment variables including system secrets. ## TypeScript IntelliSense for Custom Env Variables By default, Vite provides type definitions for `import.meta.env` in `vite/client.d.ts`. To get IntelliSense for custom `VITE_` variables, augment the `ImportMetaEnv` interface: ```typescript // src/vite-env.d.ts (or env.d.ts — any .d.ts file in your project) /// interface ImportMetaEnv { readonly VITE_APP_TITLE: string; readonly VITE_API_URL: string; readonly VITE_PUBLIC_API_KEY: string; readonly VITE_DEBUG: string; // env values are always strings readonly VITE_FEATURE_FLAGS: string; } interface ImportMeta { readonly env: ImportMetaEnv; } ``` Make sure this file is included in your `tsconfig.json`: ```json { "compilerOptions": { "types": ["vite/client"] }, "include": ["src"] } ``` **Important:** All env values are strings at runtime. Even if you write `VITE_PORT=3000` in your `.env` file, `import.meta.env.VITE_PORT` is the string `"3000"`, not the number `3000`. Parse numbers and booleans explicitly: ```typescript const port = Number(import.meta.env.VITE_PORT); const debug = import.meta.env.VITE_DEBUG === 'true'; ``` ## Using Env Variables in HTML Vite supports replacing env variables in HTML files using the `%ENV_VAR_NAME%` syntax: ```html %VITE_APP_TITLE%
``` The replacement happens both in dev (on the fly) and during build (static replacement). If a variable is not defined, it is left as-is rather than replaced with an empty string. Only `VITE_`-prefixed variables and the built-in constants (`MODE`, `BASE_URL`, `PROD`, `DEV`, `SSR`) are available for HTML replacement. ## Custom Modes The default mode is `development` for `vite dev` and `production` for `vite build`. Custom modes allow you to target specific deployment environments: ```bash # Start dev with staging mode vite --mode staging # Build for staging vite build --mode staging ``` This loads `.env.staging` and `.env.staging.local` in addition to `.env` and `.env.local`. ```bash # .env.staging VITE_APP_TITLE=My App (Staging) VITE_API_URL=https://staging-api.example.com NODE_ENV=production # Can override NODE_ENV independently of mode ``` ### Mode vs. NODE_ENV Mode and `NODE_ENV` are related but distinct concepts: | Concept | Controlled By | Affects | |---------|--------------|--------| | **Mode** | `--mode` flag or default (`development`/`production`) | Which `.env.[mode]` files are loaded | | **NODE_ENV** | Set from mode by default, can be overridden in `.env` files | Framework behavior (React dev tools, Vue devtools), `process.env.NODE_ENV` in server code, `import.meta.env.PROD`/`DEV` | By default, `vite dev` sets `NODE_ENV=development` and `vite build` sets `NODE_ENV=production`. But a custom mode like `staging` can explicitly set `NODE_ENV=production` in `.env.staging` to get production-optimized builds while still loading staging-specific config. ```bash # .env.staging — staging mode with production NODE_ENV NODE_ENV=production VITE_API_URL=https://staging-api.example.com VITE_ANALYTICS_ID=UA-STAGING-1 ``` ```bash # Build for staging: mode=staging, NODE_ENV=production vite build --mode staging ``` **Important:** `import.meta.env.PROD` and `import.meta.env.DEV` derive from `NODE_ENV`, not from `mode`. So a staging build with `NODE_ENV=production` will have `PROD=true` and `DEV=false`. ## loadEnv — Accessing Env Variables in vite.config `import.meta.env` is NOT available inside `vite.config.ts` because the config file is processed before env variables are resolved. Use the `loadEnv` helper instead: ```typescript import { defineConfig, loadEnv } from 'vite'; export default defineConfig(({ mode }) => { // Load env variables for the current mode // Third arg '' means load ALL env vars, not just VITE_-prefixed ones const env = loadEnv(mode, process.cwd(), ''); return { // Use env.VITE_API_URL, env.DATABASE_URL, etc. define: { __APP_VERSION__: JSON.stringify(env.VITE_APP_VERSION), }, server: { proxy: { '/api': { target: env.VITE_API_TARGET, changeOrigin: true, }, }, }, }; }); ``` ### loadEnv Signature ```typescript function loadEnv( mode: string, // Current mode ('development', 'production', 'staging', etc.) envDir: string, // Directory to search for .env files (usually process.cwd()) prefixes?: string | string[] // Default: 'VITE_'. Use '' to load ALL variables. ): Record ``` The `prefixes` parameter controls which variables are returned: - `'VITE_'` (default) — only `VITE_`-prefixed variables - `''` (empty string) — ALL variables from .env files - `['VITE_', 'PUBLIC_']` — variables matching any listed prefix **Security note:** When using `loadEnv` with `''` prefix in the config, be careful not to pass secrets into `define` or other options that embed values into client code. ## Variable Expansion Vite uses [dotenv-expand](https://github.com/motdotla/dotenv-expand) to expand variables within `.env` files: ```bash # .env HOST=example.com PROTOCOL=https PORT=3000 # Reference other variables with $VAR or ${VAR} VITE_BASE_URL=$PROTOCOL://$HOST VITE_FULL_URL=${PROTOCOL}://${HOST}:${PORT}/api # Escape with \ to use literal $ VITE_PRICE=\$9.99 ``` Expansion resolves across .env files in loading order, so a variable defined in `.env` can be referenced in `.env.development`. ## Common Patterns ### Feature Flags ```bash # .env.development VITE_FEATURE_NEW_DASHBOARD=true VITE_FEATURE_BETA_API=true # .env.production VITE_FEATURE_NEW_DASHBOARD=true VITE_FEATURE_BETA_API=false ``` ```typescript if (import.meta.env.VITE_FEATURE_NEW_DASHBOARD === 'true') { // show new dashboard } ``` ### Conditional Proxy in Dev ```typescript // vite.config.ts — use loadEnv to configure proxy from .env import { defineConfig, loadEnv } from 'vite'; export default defineConfig(({ mode }) => { const env = loadEnv(mode, process.cwd(), ''); return { server: { proxy: { '/api': env.VITE_API_TARGET || 'http://localhost:3001', }, }, }; }); ``` ### Validate Required Variables at Startup ```typescript // src/env.ts — centralized env validation const requiredVars = [ 'VITE_API_URL', 'VITE_PUBLIC_API_KEY', ] as const; for (const key of requiredVars) { if (!import.meta.env[key]) { throw new Error(`Missing required environment variable: ${key}`); } } export const env = { apiUrl: import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL, publicApiKey: import.meta.env.VITE_PUBLIC_API_KEY, isDev: import.meta.env.DEV, mode: import.meta.env.MODE, } as const; ``` ## Static Replacement at Build Time Vite replaces `import.meta.env.*` references with static values at [[vite/build]] time using string substitution. This means: - Dead-code elimination works — `if (import.meta.env.PROD)` blocks are removed in development, and `if (import.meta.env.DEV)` blocks are removed in production builds - Dynamic access does NOT work — `const key = 'VITE_FOO'; import.meta.env[key]` will not resolve - Destructuring does NOT work — `const { VITE_FOO } = import.meta.env` will not resolve in the build output - Always use the full static string: `import.meta.env.VITE_FOO` ```typescript // CORRECT — statically analyzable const url = import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL; // WRONG — dynamic access, will be undefined in production build const key = 'VITE_API_URL'; const url = import.meta.env[key]; // WRONG — destructuring, will break in production const { VITE_API_URL } = import.meta.env; ```

sections.title: Built-in env constants
sections.body: Vite exposes five built-in constants on `import.meta.env` without configuration: `MODE` (string — 'development', 'production', or custom), `BASE_URL` (string — from the `base` config in [[vite/config]]), `PROD` (boolean — true when NODE_ENV is 'production'), `DEV` (boolean — inverse of PROD), and `SSR` (boolean — true in server-side rendering). These are always available and do not require the VITE_ prefix. PROD and DEV derive from NODE_ENV, not from mode — so a custom mode like 'staging' with NODE_ENV=production will have PROD=true.

sections.title: .env file loading order and precedence
sections.body: Vite loads .env files using dotenv from the project root (or envDir). Loading order from highest to lowest priority: `.env.[mode].local` > `.env.[mode]` > `.env.local` > `.env`. All matching files are merged; higher-priority files override lower ones. Files ending in `.local` are git-ignored by convention and used for personal overrides. The mode defaults to 'development' for `vite dev` and 'production' for `vite build`. Variables already present in the shell environment are NOT overwritten by .env files. Format supports comments (#), quoted values (single/double), and multi-line values in double quotes.

sections.title: VITE_ prefix and client-side security
sections.body: Only environment variables prefixed with `VITE_` are exposed to client-side code via `import.meta.env`. This is a security boundary — variables without the prefix (DATABASE_URL, SECRET_KEY, etc.) remain server-side only and never appear in the JavaScript bundle. Never put secrets in VITE_ variables — they are embedded as plain text in the build output. The prefix can be customized via `envPrefix` in [[vite/config]] (e.g., `envPrefix: ['VITE_', 'PUBLIC_']`). Never set envPrefix to an empty string as that would expose all environment variables including system secrets.

sections.title: TypeScript IntelliSense for env vars
sections.body: Vite ships type definitions for built-in `import.meta.env` constants in `vite/client.d.ts`. To add IntelliSense for custom VITE_ variables, create a `vite-env.d.ts` (or `env.d.ts`) file with `/// <reference types="vite/client" />` and augment the `ImportMetaEnv` interface with your variable declarations. All env values are strings at runtime regardless of how they appear in .env files — declare them as `readonly VITE_MY_VAR: string`. Make sure the declaration file is included in your tsconfig.json `include` array. The `ImportMeta` interface can also be augmented to extend the `env` property.

sections.title: Custom modes and the --mode flag
sections.body: Beyond the default 'development' and 'production' modes, Vite supports custom modes via `--mode <name>` (e.g., `vite build --mode staging`). A custom mode loads `.env.staging` and `.env.staging.local` files. Mode and NODE_ENV are distinct: mode controls which .env files load, while NODE_ENV controls framework optimizations and the PROD/DEV constants. A staging build can set `NODE_ENV=production` in `.env.staging` to get optimized output while still using staging-specific API URLs. This pattern is common for multi-environment deployments (dev, staging, QA, production).

sections.title: Environment variable replacement in HTML
sections.body: Vite replaces `%VITE_MY_VAR%` placeholders in `index.html` with the corresponding env variable values. This works for all VITE_-prefixed variables and built-in constants (MODE, BASE_URL). Replacement occurs both during development (on the fly) and at build time (statically). If a referenced variable is not defined, the placeholder is left as-is. Use this for dynamic page titles (`<title>%VITE_APP_TITLE%</title>`), meta tags, data attributes, or any HTML content that varies by environment.

sections.title: loadEnv helper for vite.config
sections.body: `import.meta.env` is not available inside `vite.config.ts` because the config is processed before env resolution. Use Vite's `loadEnv(mode, envDir, prefixes)` helper instead. The function accepts the current mode (from the config function argument), the directory to search (usually `process.cwd()`), and an optional prefix filter (default 'VITE_', use '' for all variables). When using empty prefix, be careful not to pass secrets into `define` or other client-exposed config options. The config must use the function form of defineConfig (`defineConfig(({ mode }) => { ... })`) to access the mode parameter.

sections.title: Variable expansion with dotenv-expand
sections.body: Vite uses dotenv-expand to resolve variable references within .env files. Reference other variables with `$VAR` or `${VAR}` syntax. Escape literal dollar signs with `\$`. Expansion resolves across .env files in loading order — a base variable in `.env` can be referenced in `.env.development`. This enables composition patterns like `VITE_FULL_URL=$PROTOCOL://$HOST:$PORT/api` where the components are defined separately and may vary by environment.

sections.title: Static replacement at build time
sections.body: Vite replaces `import.meta.env.*` with static values at [[vite/build]] time via string substitution. This enables dead-code elimination — `if (import.meta.env.DEV) { ... }` is removed entirely in production builds. However, dynamic property access (`import.meta.env[key]`) and destructuring (`const { VITE_FOO } = import.meta.env`) do NOT work because the bundler cannot statically analyze them. Always use the full static form: `import.meta.env.VITE_FOO`. This also means env values are baked into the bundle and cannot be changed at runtime without rebuilding.

links.context: defineConfig, envPrefix, envDir options, loadEnv usage in config
Env vars are statically replaced at build time; --mode flag for build targets
Dev server mode defaults to 'development'; proxy config from env vars
Debug undefined env vars, missing VITE_ prefix, mode mismatches

related.note: loadEnv is used in vite.config.ts; envPrefix and envDir are config options
Env variables are statically replaced during production builds
Dev server defaults to 'development' mode for env loading
tsconfig.json must include the vite-env.d.ts declaration file

author: dojo-community
